coredata.dc:description: Cyanophyta blooms can lead to eutrophication and generate algal toxins. To reduce their environmental risks, hydrothermal carbonization was innovatively applied to simultaneously produce activated carbon (AC) and carbon dots (CDs). AC was further used for CO2 adsorption while CDs were for preparing fluorescent materials. Yields of AC and CDs were investigated under mild conditions of 160–240 °C and 15–240 min. Characterization results show that AC exhibited a large specific surface area of 990 m2 g−1 and pore volume of 1.14 cm3 g−1, contributing to high CO2 adsorption capacities of 3.26 mmol g−1 (0 °C) and 2.06 mmol g−1 (25 °C). Additionally, nitrogen and oxygen self-doping heteroatoms CDs, with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 2.58 %, emitted stable blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet light and were successfully applied to produce invisible ink. This work provides a paradigm for CO2 reduction and high-value material synthesis via the thermochemical conversion of hazardous biomass.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cyanophyta blooms can lead to eutrophication and generate algal toxins. To reduce their environmental risks, hydrothermal carbonization was innovatively applied to simultaneously produce activated carbon (AC) and carbon dots (CDs). AC was further used for CO2 adsorption while CDs were for preparing fluorescent materials. Yields of AC and CDs were investigated under mild conditions of 160–240 °C and 15–240 min. Characterization results show that AC exhibited a large specific surface area of 990 m2 g−1 and pore volume of 1.14 cm3 g−1, contributing to high CO2 adsorption capacities of 3.26 mmol g−1 (0 °C) and 2.06 mmol g−1 (25 °C). Additionally, nitrogen and oxygen self-doping heteroatoms CDs, with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 2.58 %, emitted stable blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet light and were successfully applied to produce invisible ink. This work provides a paradigm for CO2 reduction and high-value material synthesis via the thermochemical conversion of hazardous biomass.

ce:section.ce:para: As hazardous microalgae, the bloom of Cyanophyta (CY) has led to various environmental issues, including decreased levels of dissolved oxygen, water turbidity, emission of unpleasant odors, and death of fishes, significantly impacting both aquatic ecosystems and the aesthetic appeal of the water environment. Additionally, microcystin toxins released by Cyanophyta inhibit the synthesis of protein phosphatases and cellular frameworks, thereby causing the human symptoms of nausea, vomiting, skin allergies, and in severe cases, dyspnea and respiratory distress [1]. Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) is a thermochemical technique which can efficiently convert biomass into carbon-enriched solids, namely hydrochar (HC). This process involves reactions encompassing hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, condensation, polymerization, and aromatization [2]. Compared with pyrolysis and incineration, HTC utilizes water as a reaction medium and occurs under milder temperatures (180–280 °C) and pressures (2–10 MPa) [3]. Cyanophyta, with a moisture content over 97 wt% [4] can be directly processed by HTC, eliminating the energy-intensive pre-drying process. High temperatures during HTC also promote the degradation of microcystins. Degradation rates for the microcystins RR, YR, LR, LY, and LW have been reported to exceed 90 % at temperatures above 175 °C [5]. Therefore, HTC served as an effective solution for the proper disposal and resource utilization of Cyanophyta.

HC, as the primary product from HTC, exhibits elevated carbon contents, high energy density, and stable functional groups over the surface [6]. After thermal treatment or activation, HC demonstrates excellent adsorption properties, with surface functional groups capable of adsorbing pigments, heavy metals, and nutrients (NH3 and PO4 3-) in wastewater [7]. Besides these pollutants, with the escalating impact of CO2 on global warming, utilizations of HC for CO2 capture are also gaining widespread attention. Ibrahim et al. [8] used bromeliad peel waste as the feedstock to prepare CO2 adsorbents by HTC. The optimal adsorption capacity was 2.04 mmol g−1 at 30 °C and 0.1 MPa. Jiang et al. [9] performed HTC of Chlorella to prepare self-N-O doped HC. After the addition of bio-oil during HTC and subsequent KOH activation, this porous carbon demonstrated a significant CO2 uptake of 5.57 mmol g−1 at 0 °C and 0.1 MPa. Similar to Chlorella, Cyanophyta is also rich in nitrogen and might be a promising feedstock for CO2 adsorbents.

Aqueous phase products (APs), as the by-product from HTC, have high contents of carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and minerals. APs also contain a certain level of toxicity and cannot be directly discharged. Conventional methods for APs treatment include anaerobic digestion, wet oxidation, algae cultivation, and reuse [10]. Techno-economic analyses indicate that the cost of treating APs may constitute up to 90 % of the overall costs for handling hydrothermal wastes [11]. Obtaining high-value products from APs is an alternative and economic approach. Carbon dots (CDs) represent a novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial comprising a carbon framework with sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon cores. Their remarkable physical, chemical, and optical properties, along with low photobleaching, limited cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, have garnered significant attention [12]. The synthesis process for CDs includes top-down and bottom-up approaches [13]. The former involves a series of fragmentation processes, breaking down bulk materials into a 0D structure. Due to the randomness of fracture sites, this method lacks the precise control over the dimension and morphology of CDs. Complex techniques, including laser etching and electrochemical oxidation are generally required for this approach [14]. Conversely, the later involves the self-assembly and polymerization of small molecular precursors, synthesizing large molecular intermediates that undergo decomposition and carbonization to generate 0D carbon quantum dots. Techniques encompassed within this approach include microwave, pyrolysis, chemical oxidation, and hydrothermal methods. Microwave-assisted methods consume high energy, pyrolysis requires additional chemical reagents, while chemical oxidation yields nonuniform CDs in size distributions [15]. In contrast, the hydrothermal method exhibits unique advantages due to its low cost, environmental friendliness, non-toxic property, and ability to synthesize CDs from various precursors [16]. Lin et al. [17] synthesized fluorescent CQDs via HTC of sludge, which produced blue fluorescence at 450 nm under UV illumination. Furthermore, it is reported that CDs modifications with heteroatoms exhibit enhanced optical performance [18]. Li et al. [19] utilized Dunaliella salina as the feedstock for hydrothermal reactions, resulting in N/S doped CDs and exhibiting excellent photostability, low toxicity, and excitation-dependent emission. APs resulting from HTC of Cyanophyta is also abundant in heteroatoms, such as oxygen and nitrogen, contributing to enhanced electron transfer and stronger light adsorption [20], thereby making APs ideal precursors for synthesizing CDs. According to our knowledge, no prior investigations are available on HTC of Cyanophyta, especially for producing CO2 adsorbents and CDs.

In this study, HTC technology was performed to simultaneously convert high-protein and low-lipid Cyanophyta into CO2 adsorbents and fluorescent CDs. Various reaction temperatures (160–240 °C) and reaction times (15–240 min) were investigated to reveal their impacts on yields of HC, activated carbon (AC), and CDs. AC and CDs under 200 °C and 30 min were comprehensively characterized for their morphology, pore structure, size distribution, functional group. Potential applications of products including CO2 capture and invisible ink were also evaluated.

Cyanophyta, the feedstock for HTC, was collected from Lake Taihu, China. For the convenience of loading, Cyanophyta was dried for 24 h in an 80 °C oven (DHG-9030A) and ground to 150 mesh. Deionized water utilized throughout the experiment was prepared by an ultra-pure water system (KZ-CCS). KOH (AR) and HCl (AR), used for the activation of HC, were both procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Table 1 summarizes the elemental analysis and biochemical composition.

In Fig. 1 , a 50 mL batch reactor equipped with a magnetic stirrer (NSSD-P5-T3-SS1-SV-R-E; Anhui Kemi Instrument Co., Ltd.) was used to perform the HTC reaction. 3 g of powdered Cyanophyta and 30 mL of deionized water were carefully loaded into the reactor. After nitrogen purging and being sealed, the magnetic stirring speed was set at 200 rpm for mixture. Subsequently, the reactor was elevated to a given temperature (160, 180, 200, 220, 240 °C). The reaction temperature was measured by a thermocouple and maintained for a specified reaction time (15, 30, 60, 90, 120 min). After cooling, products were transferred to a centrifuge tube and separated by centrifuging (TG16-WS) at 8000 rpm for 7 min. The upper layer was regarded as APs. The remaining solids were dried at 105 °C for 24 h to produce HC. HC was weighed to determine its yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical activation to obtain activated carbon
ce:section.ce:para: The HC was chemically activated via an impregnation method. HC and KOH solution (0.9 mol L−1) with the ratios of 1:1 (w/w) were mixed at 60 °C for 2 h, and then dried at 105 °C for another 10 h. The activation of the dried solids occurred under an N2 atmosphere with a horizontal tube furnace (CHY-1200). The temperature of the furnace ramped at 10 °C·min−1 to 700 °C and was maintained for 2 h. Subsequently, the activated solids were neutralized in a 1 mol L−1 HCl solution for 3 h, and then rinsed by deionized water until the of the pH of the rinsing water reached 7.0. The sample was dried at 105 °C for 10 h and labelled as AC. AC was also weighed to determine its yield.

The APs containing carbon dots (CDs) were placed into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da (JELUP, USA). The dialysis bag was transferred into a water-filled beaker. DI water was changed every 4 h to remove the low-molecular compounds. After being dialyzed for 24 h, a solution of CDs was prepared. The CDs solution was lyophilized with a vacuum freeze dryer (YTLG-10C) for 48 h. The resulting CDs powder was collected and weighed. All experiments were conducted in triplicate. The average value and the standard deviation were presented.

The Unicube element analysis system was employed to analyze the ultimate composition of the Cyanophyta in Table 1. Higher heating value (HHV) was determined by an automatic calorimeter (ZDHW-6000). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Nexus, Thermo Nicolet Co., America) was used to identify the functional groups of CY, HC, AC and CDs over the wavelength range from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. Each spectrum was an average of 36 scans. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) was used to analyze the functional groups on the surface of AC and CDs. Particularly, AC and CDs were mounted on the sample holder and placed into the sample chamber when the pressure was less than 2.0 × 10−7 mbar. Survey spectra were conducted at a spot size of 400 μm and a pass energy of 150.0 eV. Peaks were fitted by Avantage.

Morphologies of Cyanophyta, HC, and AC were obtained under a ZEISS Sigma 300 field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM). The gas adsorption/desorption isotherms of N2 (−196 °C, 0 °C, and 25 °C) and CO2 (−78 °C, 0 °C, and 25 °C) were investigated by a specific surface area and micropore analyzer (Model: BSD-PM, BeiShiDe Instrument, China). Before the gas adsorption experiment, all samples were dried at 105 °C for 3 h to remove water, and then degassed at 200 °C for 6 h. The Brunauer-Emmet-teller (BET) isotherm model was applied to calculate BET surface area (S BET). The total pore volume (V total) was acquired by the total adsorption amount of gas at P/P 0 = 0.99. The surface area (S t-micro) and the volume (V t-micro) of micropores were calculated by t-plot method. The average pore diameter (D average) was calculated by BET method. Pore size distributions (PSD) of AC were acquired from the adsorption isotherms of N2 (−196 °C) and CO2 (−78 °C) with the non-local density functional theory (NLDFT) slit pore model. The adsorption capacities of AC for CO2 and N2 were measured by the static capacity method at both 0 °C and 25 °C. The regeneration performance of CO2 adsorption was initially degassed at 200 °C for 6 h and then degassed at 25 °C for 1 h from the second to fifth cycle.

The quantum yield of the CDs was measured with quinine sulfate as the reference. Particle size distribution of CDs in its aqueous solution was analyzed using a size and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, UK). Morphologies of CDs were captured with a transmission electron microscope (TEM; HT-7800; Hitachi, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 120 kV. The obtained CDs solution was diluted by factors of 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50. A UV lamp (SHENYU, China) with a wavelength of 365 nm was used to illuminate the CD solution from top to bottom. The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum was measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600i Plus, Japan). Fluorescence excitation spectra were measured with an FLS1000 steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK) at excitation wavelengths of 300 nm, 350 nm, and 400 nm. To prepare a fluorescent ink, 100 mg of CDs powder was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and mixed with 5 mL of glycerol as a thickening agent.

Fig. 2 shows the effects of temperature and reaction time on the yields of HC, AC, and CDs. As depicted in the column of Fig. 2 (a), HC yields gradually decreased from 55.4 wt% to 35.2 wt% as the reaction temperature rose from 180 °C to 240 °C. This trend aligned with Khoo et al.’s study [22] on HTC of Chlorella. Higher temperatures promoted the hydrolysis and degradation of Cyanophyta, leading to the generation of liquid compounds and thereby the reduction of HC yield [23]. Similar to the variation of HC, with the elevation of reaction temperature, AC yields initially rose and then declined, reaching its peak of 6.49 % at 200 °C. This phenomenon was attributed to the progressive increase in the polymerization degree and carbonization level of HC with the rising temperature, which promoted the generation of activation precursors of HC [24]. However, with a further increase in temperature, HC released more volatile components when activated with KOH, thereby lowering the post-activation carbon yield [25]. In Fig. 2 (a), with the temperature below 200 °C, the change in CDs yield was modest, peaking at 3.6 wt%. Further raising the temperature from 200 °C to 220 °C, the CDs yield significantly dropped to 1.1 wt%. Elevated temperatures promoted the degradation reaction and produced low-molecular-weight products [18]. These low-molecular substances were removed during the dialysis stage.

Fig. 2 (b) shows the influence of reaction time on product yields at 200 °C. 200 °C was selected as this temperature gave the highest yields of AC and CDs. HC yield initially decreased from 56.5 wt% to 48.4 wt% before 30 min, and with little variations after 30 min. Alcazar-Ruiz et al. [26] also observed the similar trend on HTC of almond shells. Additionally, variations of AC and CDs displayed opposite trends with the prolonged reaction time in Fig. 2 (b). Extending the time from 15 min to 120 min, AC marginally increased from with 6.3 wt% to 7.8 wt% due to the enhanced polymerization and carbonization reactions. However, CDs yields dramatically dropped from 7.8 wt% to 2.3 % within this time range. It was reported that prolonged reaction time contributed to the re-aggregation or fragmentation of CDs, leading to a decrease in CDs yield [18]. By considering the total yield of AC and CDs, along with the fluorescence characteristics of CDs discussed later, 200 °C and 30 min were chosen for the following characterization experiments.

Fig. 3 (a)–(c) display the FE-SEM images of Cyanophyta, HC, and AC. The surface of Cyanophyta was compact, presenting a wavy and wrinkled morphology with no pores. After HTC of Cyanophyta, HC obtained exhibited numerous irregular folds featuring a limited number of pores. After activation with KOH, the AC presented a uniform and honeycomb-like surface, contributing to larger surface areas and pore volumes. The development of pores on the HC surface was associated with the reaction between KOH and carbon, resulting in the formation of K2O, K2CO3, metallic K, etc. [27]. The release of H2, the decomposition of K2CO3, and the intensified carbon gasification, as shown in Eqs. (5)–(7) all induced the development of pore structures in AC. The sponge-like morphology and dense pore structure on the surface of AC suggested its favorable adsorption capacity for CO2.
(5) 6KOH+2C→2K+3H2+2K2CO3
(6) K2CO3→K2O + CO2
(7) CO2+C→2CO

Fig. 3 (d) shows the adsorption/desorption isotherms of N2 and CO2 while Fig. 3 (e) depicts the distribution of pore sizes in AC. Based on the IUPAC classification, AC exhibits mixed type I and type Ⅱ isotherms and H3 hysteresis loops. All stages of micropore filling at very low pressures, monolayer adsorption at low pressures, multilayer adsorption at medium pressures, and capillary condensation at high pressures could be observed in Fig. 3 (d). These isotherms indicated the co-existence of micropores and mesopores, which was consistent with the pore size of AC. The sharp increase at very low pressures indicated the microporous structure of AC, while the dramatical increase at high pressures and the hysteresis loop suggested the formation of uniformly split mesopores. As shown in Fig. 3 (e), although some mesopores were present with the pore diameter of 2.5 nm, due to the KOH activation, most pore diameters of AC ranged from 0.4 nm to 1 nm. These narrow micropores would offer an extensive surface area and reduced diffusion paths for CO2 adsorption [28].

The pore parameters of Cyanophyta, HC and AC were provided in Table 2 . After the activation of KOH, the S BET increased from 6.5453 m2 g−1 to 990 m2 g−1 and the V total rose from 0.0427 cm3 g−1 to 1.1439 cm3 g−1, indicating a significant enhancement in the porosity of AC. Additionally, in Table 2, the S t-micro and V t-micro of Cyanophyta and HC were 0, while those of AC increased to 797 m2 g−1 and 0.3294 cm3 g−1, respectively. Due to the high ash content Cyanophyta, S BET reported in this study was lower than that of Chlorella-derived AC [9]. However, micropores in Cyanophyta-derived AC accounted for about 80 % of the total specific surface area. Differences between S BET of
AC
N 2
and
A
C
C
O 2
also demonstrated the presence of ultramicropores with a diameter below 0.8 nm [29]. These micropores, especially the ultramicropores, were beneficial for CO2 adsorption.

The FT-IR spectra of CY, HC, and AC are depicted in Fig. 4 (a) . Functional groups of CY and HC were similar. Dried Cyanophyta powder had a much stronger absorption band than HC and AC did, which was attributed to the breakage of chemical bonds, release of volatile compounds, and decomposition of macromolecules during HTC and activation [30]. The absence of peaks (e.g., -CH and C=O) in AC, compared to CY and HC, might be attributed to the porous morphology of AC, causing the absorption of IR light and rendering functional group peaks undetectable [31]. CY, HC, and AC all had broad peaks at 3400 cm−1, which was ascribed to the -OH or the -NH stretching vibrations. A signal at 3400 cm−1 indicated the existence of saccharides and aromatic secondary amine. The intensity of this peak in AC was weaker than that in HC, suggesting the enhanced hydrophobicity of AC with the decrease in the surface hydroxyl. Peaks at approximately 2925 cm−1, representing the -CH2 antisymmetric stretching vibration, were only observed in CY and HC due to the disappearance of long-chain alkyl with deeper activation. The distinctive band revealed by AC near 2084 cm−1 indicated the existence of C ≡ C functional group [32]. In CY, the peak at 1655 cm−1 was related to the C=O stretching vibrations in aromatic ketones, aromatic aldehydes and carbonyls. However, this peaks disappeared in HC and AC due to the decarboxylation reaction during HTC or activation [27]. Instead, HC and AC exhibited bending vibrations of NH2 at 1629 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1, respectively, showing the existence of amides. The signals at 1547 cm−1 in CY and at 1540 cm−1 in HC could be assigned to the C-N-H bending vibration in amides. There were weak peaks at 1456 cm−1 in both CY and HC, which were ascribed to the vibration of C-H asymmetric angular variation. The peaks at approximately 1400 cm−1 in CY, HC, and AC could be assigned to the C-N stretching vibrations of amides. Peaks at 1039 cm−1 for CY, 1038 cm−1 for HC, and 1096 cm−1 for AC indicated the C-O stretching vibrations in the esters or alcohols. Peaks at 533 cm−1 for CY and HC corresponded to the out-of-plane wagging vibrations of COO−. The peaks at approximately 470 cm−1 for all samples corresponded to deviational vibration of SO4 2-. FT-IR results demonstrate the presence of O/N/S heteroatoms in the prepared AC, which played significant roles in CO2 adsorption [33].

Fig. 4(b–e) shows the XPS analysis results of HC and AC. The XPS full-scan spectra in Fig. 4 (b) revealed distinct signals at approximately 284, 400, and 532 eV. The C 1s spectrum of HC in Fig. 4 (c) exhibited three peaks with binding energies of 284.8, 286.16 and 288.26 eV, which were attributed to C-C, C-O, and C=O, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 4 (d), the N 1s spectrum of HC could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 398.45 eV, 399.63 eV, 400.41 eV, and 401.69 eV, representing pyridinic-N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and oxidized N, respectively. In Fig. 4 (e), the fitted O 1s spectrum of HC corresponded to C=O (531.63 eV) and C-OH (532.71 eV), aligning with the findings from FTIR analysis in Fig. 4 (a).

Compared with HC, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of AC (Fig. 4 (c)) shows an additional peak of O-C=O at 289.01 eV, while in the N 1s spectrum of AC (Fig. 4 (d)), the peak of graphitic-N disappeared. Pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N, as the main nitrogen species in AC, were effective binding sites of CO2 molecules. Pyrrolic-N contributed more significantly to CO2 adsorption compared with pyridinic-N and graphitic N [34] by providing stronger hydrogen bonds between C-H bonds and CO2 [35]. Therefore, it was favorable that the proportion of pyrrolic-N exceeded that of pyridinic-N in AC. Compared with HC, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum of AC presented new characteristic peaks of 533.72 eV (R-O-C=O) and 535.31 eV (C-OOH). The oxygen ratio increased from 26.2 % to 44.0 % after activation. These oxygen atoms could enhance the hydrogen bond interaction between the AC surface and CO2 [36]. The ratio of C-OH increased from 39.8 % to 59.8 % after HC activation. As the dominant oxygen species on the surface of AC, C-OH represents the basic and hydrophilic sites of AC, promoting the capture of acidic CO2 [37].

Fig. 5 illustrates the adsorption isotherms of CO2 and N2 of AC. It was favorable that the adsorption of CO2 was notably more pronounced than that of N2 for the higher quadrupole moment and polarizability of CO2 than N2, along with the differences between the boiling point of N2 and the sublimation point of CO2 [37]. The CO2/N2 selectivities were calculated to be 21 at 0 °C and 20 at 25 °C (see the supplementary material for details), which were higher than those of AC from HTC of tobacco stalk-derived carbon [28]. In particular, the CO2 uptake at 25 °C and 0 °C for AC was 2.06 mmol g−1 and 3.26 mmol g−1, respectively. A reduced CO2 adsorption capacity at 25 °C was attributed to the faster escape velocity from the active sites or pores in AC, the inhibition of multilayer adsorption, and the exothermic characteristic of physical adsorption of CO2 [38,39].

In addition to the adsorption capacity, the recyclability of AC was also crucial for the long-term application. Fig. 5 (c) depicts the 5 cycles of CO2 adsorption/desorption at 25 °C. During rapid desorption within 1 h, the adsorption capacity of AC could be easily regenerated and was stable during each run. This indicated that it was a physisorption process instead of a chemisorption process [27]. The weak interaction between of AC and CO2 eliminated the need for long-time and energy-consuming heat regeneration.

Table 3 compares the CO2 adsorption capacities of AC prepared from HTC of different algae at 1 bar. It was noted that in this research, the adsorption performance of AC derived solely from HTC of Cyanophyta without adding N-doping agents. The CO2 adsorption capacities obtained in our study were 3.26 mmol g−1 at 0 °C and 2.06 mmol g−1 at 25 °C, exceeding those of AC from HTC of E. prolifera [40], E. prolifera with melamine as N source [41], and Chlorella sp [42]. However, the uptakes in this study were slightly lower than those derived from Chlorella vulgaris [43] due to the lower nitrogen content of Cyanophyta. CO2 adsorption capacity primarily depended on the ratio of micropores and the surface nitrogen content [9]. High nitrogen content in the feedstock would provide the potential for preparing high-quality N-doped AC, thereby enhancing the CO2 adsorption capacity. Additionally, in the present research, the mass ratio of HC to activator was 1:1, which was comparatively lower than most ratios reported in Table 3. A reduced ratio of HC and activator contributed to a lower activation cost.

Fig. 6 depicts the morphology, size distribution, and surface groups of CDs. CDs observed by TEM were well-dispersed with a uniform quasi-spherical shape, suggesting the existence of hydrophilic moieties on the surface of CDs, such as O-H identified by FT-IR. Good dispersibility of CDs in solvents supported their performance in fields of fluorescence probes, biochemistry, etc. In Fig. 6 (b), the particle size of CDs tested by TEM ranged from 10 nm to 90 nm, with an average diameter of 42.6 nm. This average particle size was smaller than those derived from HTC of Pectinodesmus sp. (average diameter of 67 nm), bovine gelatin (average diameter of 59 nm) [44], and Spirulina (20–50 nm) [45]. Large CDs with a diameter of approximately 90 nm were also noted, which was ascribed to the overlapping and aggregation of CDs. However, in Fig. 6 (c), the particle size analysis (PSA) of the CDs suspension indicated an average diameter of 140 nm, being significantly larger than their solid particles characterized by TEM. This discrepancy may be explained by the fact that PSA measurements captured the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs [46]. Hydrophilic functional groups (e.g. O-H) on the surfaces of CDs would significantly improve the hydrodynamic diameter. This phenomenon was also observed for CDs derived from Spirulina [45] and Chlorella [18].

Fig. 6 (d) shows the strong and broad absorption bands at 3420 cm−1 for CDs and at 3468 cm−1 for CD solution. The stretching vibrations of -OH or -NH might overlap in this range, suggesting the high hydrophilicity and N-doping of CDs. -OH also suggested the partial oxidation of CDs during HTC, which was responsible for surface functionalization [47]. A small peak at 2936 cm−1 in the CDs corresponded to the stretching vibrations of -CH2 and -CH3. The C-based structure would enhance the stability of CDs [48]. Obvious peaks at 1648, 1409, 1251 and 1151 cm−1 in CDs belong to the C=O stretching and C-N stretching of the C(=O)NH2 group. The presence of the C(=O)NH2 group contributed to the generation of blue emission bands [49]. The CD solution also had an absorption peak of the C=O stretching vibration at 1637 cm−1. The signal of CDs at 1047 cm−1 indicated the C-O bending vibration of CDs, assisting the coverage and stabilization of CDs [50]. The peak of CDs at 898 cm−1 denoted to the out-of-plane bending vibration of = CH2. The absorption band at 618 cm−1, representing the asymmetric deviational vibration of SO4 2-, was also observed, verifying the presence of sulfur in CDs [18]. From the FT-IR results, it was noted that both CDs and CDs solutions were rich in functional groups involving O, N, and S elements, which would act as energy traps to improve the fluorescence performances of CDs [51].

Fig. 6 (e) shows the XPS full-scan spectra of CDs with distinct peaks of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The measured atomic proportions of C, N, O on the surface of CDs were 62.99 %, 4.24 %, 32.77 %, respectively. The deconvoluted XPS spectrum of the C 1s in Fig. 6 (f) revealed signals at 284.81 eV (C=C), 286.42 eV (C-O), and 288.06 eV (C=O), accounting for 35.4 %, 49.6 % and 15.0 %, respectively. C=C, especially aromatic C=C bonds in the ring structure contributed to the opticality, stability, and conductivity of CDs [48]. C-O and C=O observed by XPS were in good agreement with the previous FT-IR results. The N 1s spectrum exhibited four peaks of pyridinic N (398.67 eV), amine N (399.76 eV), pyrrolic N (400.43 eV), and graphitic N (401.80 eV), implying the doping of a small amount of nitrogen on CDs. Among them, amine-N and pyrrolic-N were the major nitrogen species in CDs, accounting for 47.9 %, and 34.2 % in CDs, respectively. N atoms, including unpaired electrons contributed to the emission characteristics of CDs [12]. Amine-N could reduce the bandgap [52] while pyrrolic-N improved the charge mobility and donor-acceptor ability of CDs, thereby contributing to the quantum yield. O 1s spectral peaks of C=O and C-OH were observed at 531.3 eV and 532.8 eV, accounting for 20.0 % and 80.0 %, respectively. Similar to the nitrogen-containing groups, oxygen-containing groups were also conducive to increasing the quantum yield by enhancing the multiphoton active process and the surface passivation [47,53].

Fig. 7 (a) shows the CD solution diluted in different multiples (10–50) at different HTC reaction times (15–120 min). The CD solution emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light, similar to the bright cyan blue light of the CD solution prepared by Morus nigra fruit under ultraviolet irradiation [54]. The fluorescence behavior of CDs could be attributed to two factors: quantum size effects and surface defect states [44]. The dominance of the quantum confinement mechanism in fluorescence behavior necessitated that the particle size of CDs be smaller than the Bohr exciton radius [55]. Due to the relatively large sizes of CDs, we infer that the photo-induced luminescence of the Cyanophyta-derived CDs in the present study was likely governed by their surface defect states. As the dilution ratio of CDs increased, the fluorescence of the solution gradually intensified. CDs in high concentration solutions usually underwent fluorescence resonance energy transfer and π-π interaction, causing the non-radiative transition and finally exhibiting the aggregation-induced quenching [56]. It could be observed that the blue fluorescence of the CDs solution at a 50-fold dilution with a reaction time of 30 min (CD-50-30) was the most prominent. Therefore, CD-50-30 was selected for UV–Vis and photoluminescence analyses.

Fig. 7 (b) illustrates the ultraviolet–visible spectrum of CD-50-30. Absorptions were mainly observed in the ultraviolet region. Obvious peaks below 300 nm were attributed to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic rings in CD-50-30, while weak absorptions over 300 nm resulted from the n-π∗ transition of C=O groups of in CD-50-30 [57]. UV–Vis irradiation would lead to more oxygen-containing surface defects and a wider energy levels, thereby inducing a broader UV–visible absorption band [58] and contributing to the fluorescence emission. Fig. 7 (b) also depicts the photoluminescence emission spectra of CD-50-30 under excitation at 300, 350, and 400 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra strongly depended on the excitation wavelength due to the various sizes, emission sites and functional groups of CDs [59]. The strongest photoluminescence emission peak appeared at 435 nm with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. 435 nm fell within the blue light region. Therefore, in Fig. 7 (a), when the 365 nm (close to 350 nm) UV lamp irradiated the CD solution, the CD solution emitted blue light. Additionally, it was noted that the highest peak of CD-50-30 was red-shifted from 430 nm to 480 nm with the increasing excitation wavelengths. Some new energy states might be created within the band gap of CDs by various functional groups [60]. The photoluminescence quantum yield of CD-50-30 was determined to be 2.58 %, surpassing that of CDs prepared through HTC of glucose, glycine, and octadecanoic acid [18].

The optical properties of CDs facilitated their applications in fluorescence sensing, printing and anti-counterfeiting. In Fig. 7 (c), the prepared fluorescent ink was used to write the initial of Cyanophyta (C) on non-fluorescent paper. Under the UV light, the letter “C” fluoresced blue light when the ink was dry. Even after constant exposure to the 365 nm UV light for 2 h, it did not reveal a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity, demonstrating the strong photobleaching resistance of the fluorescent ink. Therefore, the prepared CDs-derived ink could potentially be used for anti-counterfeiting printing or anti-counterfeiting labels.

In this study, Cyanophyta was valorized through a hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) route. The target product of HTC, hydrochar, was adopted to produce activated carbon adsorbents, while the by-product, aqueous phase products were employed to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots. The activated carbon exhibited a porous and honeycomb-like microstructure with O, N, and S heteroatoms, providing a good capacity (3.26 mmol g−1 at 0 °C) and a stable repeatability for CO2 adsorption. The particle size of the carbon dots ranged from 10 nm to 90 nm with abundant nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing groups, showing obvious blue fluorescence emission under the UV excitation. Fluorescent ink prepared from carbon dots demonstrated significant potential and feasibility in anti-counterfeiting applications.
